title: Quick check: the four contact roles

questions.prompt: During a routine audit, you find a long-term client's domain is registered with the registrant set to 'John Smith' — a staff member who left the company two years ago. What's the calibrated next step?
choices: Email John Smith (if you can reach him) and ask him to authorise a change.

Surface to the client owner: their domain's legal registrant is an ex-employee. The change-of-registrant process moves ownership to the client's business entity but typically triggers a 60-day outbound-transfer lock. Schedule it during a calm period.
Edit the registrant in the registrar panel directly to the client's company name; no notification needed.
Leave it — John Smith doesn't work there anymore so it's effectively under the company's control.
questions.explanation: The registrant is the legal owner. If John Smith ever disputes (or if a registrar-level account-recovery process is triggered), he's the named party. Changing the registrant requires an explicit change-of-registrant process at most registrars — it triggers a 60-day outbound lock and produces verification emails. Surface to the client owner first; don't quietly edit it, and don't email an ex-employee. Documenting the change is part of the work.

questions.prompt: Which statement is correct about the registrant and admin contact roles?
choices: The admin contact and the registrant are the same role; the labels are interchangeable.
The registrant owns the domain; the admin contact is authorised to make changes. They can be the same person but the roles are independent.
The admin contact owns the domain; the registrant is just the billing party.
Only one of the four contacts matters; the others are filler.
questions.explanation: The registrant has legal standing; the admin contact has operational authority. Different roles in the registry's data model, sometimes filled by the same human. All four roles serve specific purposes (ownership, authority, notifications, billing); none are filler.

questions.prompt: A transfer-out request fails because the approval email is bouncing. You check the admin contact and it's the email of someone who left the client 18 months ago. What's the fix?
choices: Re-initiate the transfer; emails sometimes bounce intermittently.
Email the previous employee at their old address asking them to forward the approval.
Update the admin contact to a live email at the client (or a shared mailbox the client controls), wait for any registry verification, then re-initiate with a fresh auth code.
Tell the client the transfer can't proceed; their previous admin contact is locked in.
questions.explanation: The address is dead, not intermittent. Re-initiating without fixing the contact produces the same failure. Admin contacts are editable; nothing is locked in. The fix is updating the contact first, then re-initiating.
